origin: A short line in Koine Greek from the genealogical section of the New Testament, specifically Matthew’s account of Jesus’ lineage (Matthew 1), referencing the deportation/exile to Babylon.
author: An early Christian Gospel writer traditionally identified as “Matthew” (or a Matthean/early church source compiling the genealogical tradition).
thoughts: The author is likely trying to preserve a genealogical linkage between prior Judean kings and the line leading toward Jesus, emphasizing that this family line is connected to the Babylonian exile. The phrasing feels matter-of-fact, as if the author/scribe is compiling tradition rather than narrating events for drama—possibly to show continuity, legitimacy, and fulfillment of God’s plan through historical moments like the exile.
audience: Jewish-Christian or Jewish-oriented early believers who would recognize names and historical events (Josiah, Jechoniah, Babylonian exile) and care about ancestry and messianic legitimacy.
written_when: Probably late 1st century CE, commonly dated to around 80–90 CE (with some scholarly proposals extending earlier).
